summary: I was born in Salem, Oregon in 1935, where my father owned a restaurant called Tokio Sukiyaki. We lived there until the war, and my parents were busy running the restaurant while I was cared for by the Williams family nearby. Salem was mostly a Caucasian neighborhood, not a Japanese American community. Before we were interned, we lived at 740 Chemeketa Street in Salem, and my parents had to sell the restaurant when we left for the camps.

Tokio Sukiyaki was my father's restaurant in Salem, Oregon, where he worked hard to support our family before the war. My parents ran the restaurant while I was just a baby, so I was taken care of by the Williams family during those busy times. Unfortunately, when we were interned, my parents had to sell the restaurant, and I don't think they received any money for it. It was an important part of our life before the upheaval caused by the war.

The Williams family took me in when I was only three weeks old because my parents were busy running the restaurant. Velora, their eleven-year-old daughter, treated me like a little sister, and I grew up with them for about three or four years. Even after we were interned and moved far away, we stayed in touch through letters and visits, and Velora kept everything about my life. She passed away in 2009, but her daughter Carla helped me publish a book with Velora's writings about those years.

We were sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center when I was about three years old and stayed there for about a year before being transferred to Topaz. I don't remember much about camp life, but I did write letters to Velora, some of which were censored. The Williams family even visited us there, going through a lot of security to see us. Tule Lake was a significant part of my early childhood during the war.

After spending about a year at Tule Lake, my family was transferred to the Topaz War Relocation Center. I don't have many memories of camp life, but this move was part of the internment experience during World War II. It was a difficult time for us, uprooted from our home in Salem and living under guard. The camps shaped much of my early life and family history.

After the war, my family moved to Cleveland, Ohio, where my father found work as a cook at a fraternity house connected to Case Western Reserve University. I actually grew up in that fraternity house, and the pledges were kind enough to build swing sets for us. This was a big change from our life in Salem and the camps, but it helped us start over in a new place.

Medford, Oregon was where Velora, the Williams family's daughter, lived later in life. We visited her there several times when we were in California, and she was always excited to see me after so many years. She was interviewed by a reporter from Medford after the 9/11 attacks about her views on internment camps. Medford played a role in preserving the memory of our friendship and the history of the camps.

After the 9/11 attacks, a reporter from Medford, Oregon interviewed Velora about her thoughts on the possibility of internment camps for Muslims. She was very outspoken against the internment of Japanese Americans during World War II and expressed strong opposition to repeating such actions. This interview highlighted how the history of the camps still resonated decades later, especially through Velora's perspective.
